nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, still or slow-moving freshwater systems including streams, swamps, ponds, ditches, reservoirs, and rice paddies.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical lowland freshwater wetlands and rice-paddy / man-made still-water systems with dense vegetation.

diet.notes: The Whisker Shrimp is an opportunistic scavenger that generally accepts sinking pellets, shrimp-formulated foods, flakes, blanched vegetables such as spinach, cucumber, and zucchini, and frozen or live foods such as bloodworms or brine shrimp. It has been documented to actively hunt smaller shrimp and small or slow-moving fish.

goodWith: fast mid- and top-swimming fish (e.g. danios, larger rasboras)
robust tetras roughly the shrimp's own size
other Whisker Shrimp in larger tanks at 1 male : 1-2 females ratio
avoidWith: Amano shrimp and other small crustaceans
large aggressive cichlids and other shrimp predators

summary: The Whisker Shrimp is a translucent Southeast Asian freshwater prawn often mis-sold as ghost shrimp. It grows to around 6 cm and is recognizable by its long, slender first walking legs. Generally considered semi-aggressive, it is known to prey on smaller tankmates.
careNotes: Whisker Shrimp are widely and persistently mis-sold as ghost shrimp (Palaemonetes paludosus); buyers should look for the characteristic long, slender, whisker-like first walking legs and elongated chelae that distinguish Macrobrachium lanchesteri. They are generally considered semi-aggressive and have been reported to attack and eat dwarf shrimp, snails, and small or slow-moving fish, so a true community of small invertebrates is not recommended. Males have been known to fight each other in tight quarters, so tanks should be sized generously when keeping more than one. They are highly motivated escape artists and have been reported to climb cords, airline tubing, and silicone seams — a tightly fitting lid with no gaps is generally advised. Like other crustaceans, they are typically sensitive to copper-based medications and to dissolved heavy metals, and they require stable parameters and good iodine availability for clean molts.
breedingNotes: Breeding is generally considered moderately difficult to difficult in home aquaria. Unlike many dwarf shrimp, M. lanchesteri does not hatch fully-formed miniature shrimp — females carry eggs for roughly 3-4 weeks and release planktonic larvae that require a separate rearing tank. Reported clutches range from about 60 to 400 eggs. First-stage larvae are typically fed newly-hatched brine shrimp nauplii. A 1 male : 1-2 females ratio is commonly recommended; multiple males will tend to fight.

additional.name: Diszhal.info - Macrobrachium lanchesteri (Glass / Ghost shrimp)
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (males 6-8 cm / females 5-6 cm), temperature 18-30 C, pH 6.5-7.5, hardness 4-30, tank 40 L minimum, lifespan 3-4 yr.

additional.name: ZooKeys 2024 - Molecular phylogeny of Macrobrachium lanchesteri (Mahidol University / Pensoft)
additional.notes: Taxonomic verification (Decapoda, Caridea, Palaemonidae) and confirmation that wild M. lanchesteri is a species complex with substantial geographic / genetic variation.

crustacean.moltingNotes: The Whisker Shrimp molts at intervals typical of palaemonid prawns — generally weeks to a month between molts depending on growth rate, temperature, and feeding, though precise figures vary. Stable water parameters and adequate iodine and calcium are commonly advised for clean molts. Shed exoskeletons are typically left in the tank, as they provide a calcium and chitin source.
crustacean.breedingNotes: This species breeds in freshwater. Females carry eggs for roughly 3 to 4 weeks, then release planktonic larvae rather than fully formed miniature shrimp; the larvae require a dedicated rearing setup and live first foods such as brine shrimp nauplii. Reported clutch sizes range from around 60 to 400 eggs.
